text: FAST stroke screen: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services. Sudden numbness or weakness, especially on one side, with severe headache or speech changes is a stroke alert until proven otherwise. Call emergency services immediately.

Pediatric fever: <3 months with any fever (>38C / 100.4F) → urgent in-person evaluation. 3-36 months with persistent fever >102F or >3 days → contact pediatrician advice line. Provide hydration. Avoid aspirin in children (Reye's syndrome risk).

NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) combined with anticoagulants (warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban) significantly increase bleeding risk. Patients on anticoagulants should consult their prescribing physician or pharmacist before taking NSAIDs. Acetaminophen is generally a safer over-the-counter alternative for pain in this population.

Respiratory triage: cough >3 weeks, fever, dyspnea, chest pain, or pulse oximetry <94% warrants in-person evaluation. Distinguishing bronchitis from pneumonia requires physical exam and possibly chest imaging — diagnosis cannot be made from history alone.

Mental-health crisis: any expression of hopelessness, suicidal ideation, or self-harm requires immediate compassionate response. Provide US 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (call or text), encourage contact with a trusted person, and emphasize that help is available now. Do not minimize. Do not delay.

Chest pain red flags: pressure or squeezing, radiating to arm/jaw/back, with shortness of breath, sweating, or nausea — assume cardiac event until ruled out. Call emergency services immediately. Aspirin 325mg may be given to a conscious patient unless contraindicated.

Adult BP categories: normal <120/80, elevated 120-129/<80, stage 1 hypertension 130-139/80-89, stage 2 hypertension >=140/90, hypertensive crisis >180/120. A reading >180/120 with symptoms (chest pain, vision change, neurological symptoms) is an emergency.

General principle: this assistant provides triage guidance only — not a diagnosis or treatment. For non-urgent questions, recommend the user contact their primary-care provider. For urgent or red-flag symptoms, recommend emergency services. Always recommend consulting a clinician before changing medication.
